question: Based on this image, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Thoracic aortic dissection with false lumen

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the coronal contrast-enhanced CT image for aortic arch anatomy, the presence and appearance of any focal outpouching, and the enhancement pattern. I note the aortic arch courses to the right side of the trachea (right-sided aortic arch) and there is a rounded, contrast-opacified outpouching arising from the posterior/leftward aspect of the arch at the origin of a vessel (arrows). The outpouching fills with contrast uniformly, indicating it communicates with the arterial lumen and is vascular rather than cystic.

Next, I relate these observations to the question, which asks for the most likely diagnosis among vascular and nonvascular options. The key image features to match are: (1) a right-sided arch, (2) an aberrant vessel origin coursing posteriorly from the arch, and (3) a fusiform/rounded dilatation at that origin that enhances with contrast.

Then I consider how each option fits the image. Option A (Kommerell diverticulum from aberrant LSA) fits because the lesion is a contrast-filled dilatation at the site where an aberrant subclavian-type vessel originates from a right-sided arch and the vessel courses posteriorly. Option B (saccular aortic arch aneurysm) is less consistent because the dilatation is not a discrete saccular pouch separate from a branch origin but is located at a branch origin and is fusiform in contour. Option C (traumatic aortic isthmus pseudoaneurysm) typically involves the aortic isthmus just distal to a normal left-sided arch configuration rather than an outpouching at an aberrant branch origin on a right-sided arch. Option D (thoracic aortic dissection) would show an intimal flap or double lumen, which is not seen. Option E (esophageal duplication cyst) would be a non-enhancing cystic mass distinct from the contrast-opacified aortic lumen.

Finally, tying these points together: the image demonstrates a contrast-filled fusiform dilatation at the origin of an aberrant vessel from a right-sided aortic arch, matching the pattern described by option A.
caption:
68-year-old male with a large Kommerell diverticulum.
(a) Axial and (b) coronal post-IV contrast-enhanced MDCT showed a right-sided
aortic arch with an aberrant left subclavian artery. There was fusiform
dilatation of the origin of the aberrant left subclavian artery
(arrows), in keeping with a Kommerell diverticulum.
Note the absence of mural thrombus within the outpouching.
sub_caption: (b) coronal post-IV contrast-enhanced MDCT showed a right-sided aortic arch with an aberrant left subclavian artery. There was fusiform dilatation of the origin of the aberrant left subclavian artery (arrows), in keeping with a Kommerell diverticulum. Note the absence of mural thrombus within the outpouching.
image_context:
A 68-year-old male presented with an incidental lung
nodule detected on chest radiograph. CT thorax was performed using a
320-slice single-source scanner (Aquilion ONE; Toshiba Medical Systems
Corporation, Tochigi, Japan) with an injection of 50 ml of
a low-osmolar contrast agent, iohexol (Omnipaque 350, GE Healthcare Inc.) at
1.5 ml/s. The study showed a right-sided aortic arch
with an aberrant left subclavian artery. There was fusiform dilatation of
the origin of the aberrant left subclavian artery from the aorta, measuring
2.3 cm, in keeping with a large Kommerell diverticulum
(<xref rid="gr3" ref-type="fig">Fig.
3</xref>).).Fig. 368-year-old male with a large Kommerell diverticulum.
(a) Axial and (b) coronal post-IV contrast-enhanced MDCT showed a right-sided
aortic arch with an aberrant left subclavian artery. There was fusiform
dilatation of the origin of the aberrant left subclavian artery
(arrows), in keeping with a Kommerell diverticulum.
Note the absence of mural thrombus within the outpouching.Fig. 3
